threats.title: Genesis Depositor Reward Theft via Missing Checkpoint Update
threats.hypothesis_description: Genesis pool deposits fail to update the depositor's reward checkpoint, allowing them to retroactively claim rewards from the gauge's inception.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `_depositsForGenesis` function calls `updateReward(address(0))` instead of `updateReward(_tokenOwner)`. This leaves the user's `userRewardPerTokenPaid` at 0, meaning any subsequent call to `earned()` will calculate rewards based on the entire history of `rewardPerTokenStored`, allowing the genesis depositor to steal rewards intended for earlier stakers.
preconditions: Gauge has accumulated rewards prior to the genesis deposit
Genesis pool deposits LP tokens on behalf of a user

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Reward checkpointing is bypassed for genesis depositors
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires genesis pool to be active and approve deposits
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after a genesis deposit is made

operational_questions: Can genesis depositors immediately call getReward() after deposit?
How much value can be extracted based on historical reward rates?
validation_requirements: Test genesis deposit followed by immediate reward claim
Verify userRewardPerTokenPaid remains 0 after deposit

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for gauge to accumulate significant rewards
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Deposit into genesis pool to trigger _depositsForGenesis
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call getReward() to claim unearned historical rewards
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat or withdraw to extract stolen value
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None, can be executed atomically if genesis pool allows immediate interaction

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Legitimate stakers lose their accrued rewards
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - stolen rewards cannot be easily recovered

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Post-reward accumulation, during genesis deposit window

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large reward claims immediately following genesis deposits
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Gauge reward token balance depleting faster than expected
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Users looping genesis deposits and reward claims

threats.title: Complete Failure and Potential Fund Loss in swapExactTokensForTokensSimple
threats.hypothesis_description: The simplified swap function fails to initialize critical route struct fields, causing swaps to either revert or send funds to the zero address.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `swapExactTokensForTokensSimple`, a new `route` struct is created but `routes[0].pair` and `routes[0].receiver` are never set. `getAmountsOut` will use `address(0)` for the pair, returning 0. If `amountOutMin` is 0, the swap proceeds and sends the output tokens to `routes[0].receiver` (address(0)), resulting in permanent fund loss.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing initialization of pair and receiver fields in route struct

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Route struct is partially initialized before being passed to core routing functions

operational_questions: Does the function always revert, or can it succeed with amountOutMin=0?
Are there any frontends relying on this specific function?
validation_requirements: Test swapExactTokensForTokensSimple with amountOutMin=0
Verify destination of swapped tokens

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Router transfers user tokens to address(0) or reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: User loses funds or transaction fails

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Core routing function is completely broken
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - funds sent to address(0) are lost

threats.title: Router Swap Output Misdirection via Ignored _to Parameter
threats.hypothesis_description: The internal `_swap` function ignores the intended recipient parameter, relying entirely on user-provided route structs which may default to the zero address.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `_swap` function calculates a local `to` variable but never uses it in the actual `swap` call, instead passing `routes[i].receiver`. If a user calls `swapExactTokensForTokens` and does not explicitly set the `receiver` field in the `route` struct (e.g., using standard Solidly SDKs), the tokens will be sent to `address(0)`.

proof_of_signal.pattern: address to = ... is unused, routes[i].receiver is passed to swap()

preconditions: User calls a swap function providing a route array
User fails to populate the receiver field in the route struct

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: The _to parameter passed to _swap is dead code

operational_questions: Do standard frontends correctly populate the receiver field?
How many users are at risk of losing funds due to this API change?
validation_requirements: Test swapExactTokensForTokens with empty receiver field
Verify tokens are sent to address(0)

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User constructs transaction using legacy Solidly SDK

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users lose funds due to API mismatch
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - funds sent to address(0) are lost

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: User complaints of missing swap outputs

threats.title: Incorrect CREATE2 Address Computation in TradeHelper
threats.hypothesis_description: TradeHelper computes pair addresses using the wrong deployer and an incomplete init code hash, breaking all routing and integrations that rely on it.
threats.investigation_rationale: `TradeHelper._pairFor` uses `PairFactory` as the deployer, but pairs are actually deployed by `PairGenerator`. Furthermore, it uses `type(Pair).creationCode` for the hash, omitting the constructor arguments required by CREATE2. This guarantees that computed addresses will never match actual pair addresses.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Uses factory instead of pairGenerator and incorrect pairCodeHash

preconditions: Integrations rely on TradeHelper for pair addresses

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Address computation logic diverges from actual deployment logic

operational_questions: Which contracts rely on TradeHelper for routing?
Does GlobalRouter fail completely due to this?
validation_requirements: Compare TradeHelper.pairFor output with PairFactory.getPair
Test GlobalRouter swaps

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User attempts to route swap through GlobalRouter
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: GlobalRouter queries TradeHelper for pair address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Swap fails or interacts with malicious contract at predicted address

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: GlobalRouter and TradeHelper are completely broken

threats.title: Protocol-Wide Swap DoS via Unbounded Referral Fee
threats.hypothesis_description: The lack of an upper bound on the referral fee allows the fee manager to inadvertently or maliciously halt all trading across the protocol.
threats.investigation_rationale: `PairFactory.setReferralFee` does not enforce a maximum limit. If `MAX_REFERRAL_FEE` is set above 10000, the fee calculation in `Pair._update0` will result in `_referralFee > amount`. The subsequent `amount -= _referralFee` will underflow, causing every swap on every pair to revert.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Fee setter lacks standard bounds checking

operational_questions: Is the feeManager a multisig or EOA?
Are there monitoring systems for fee changes?
validation_requirements: Test setting fee > 10000 and executing a swap
Verify underflow revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: feeManager key is compromised or admin makes a typo
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All subsequent swaps on all pairs revert due to underflow

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete halt of all trading activity

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden drop in swap volume to zero

threats.title: Infinite Loop DoS in TradeHelper.getAmountsIn
threats.hypothesis_description: A flawed loop condition causes an infinite loop and out-of-gas revert for all calls to getAmountsIn.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `for` loop uses a `uint` counter `i` with the condition `i >= 0`. When `i` reaches 0, the decrement `i--` causes an underflow to `type(uint).max`. Since `type(uint).max >= 0` is always true, the loop never terminates, rendering the function completely unusable.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Standard solidity anti-pattern for reverse iteration

operational_questions: Are there any frontends relying on this function for quoting?
Does this break any smart contract integrations?
validation_requirements: Test getAmountsIn with a valid route
Verify out-of-gas revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User attempts to get input quote via getAmountsIn
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Transaction or call consumes all gas and reverts

threats.title: Incorrect Math in TradeHelper.getAmountInStable Breaks Input Calculations
threats.hypothesis_description: Mathematical errors in stable pool input calculations cause the helper to return wildly inaccurate values.
threats.investigation_rationale: `getAmountInStable` calculates `y_1 = reserveOut + output` instead of subtracting the output. It also scales the final result using `decimalsOut` instead of `decimalsIn`. This results in completely incorrect input amount quotes for stable pools.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint y_1 = reserveOut + output; instead of - output

operational_questions: Are frontends displaying incorrect quotes to users?
Could this lead to users executing unfavorable trades?
validation_requirements: Test getAmountInStable with known reserves and output
Compare result with actual required input

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User queries getAmountInStable for a trade
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Helper returns incorrect inflated or deflated amount
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: User executes trade based on bad quote
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Trade fails K-invariant or user suffers slippage

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users complaining about bad stable pool quotes

threats.title: WAVAX Withdrawals DoS for Smart Contracts via .transfer
threats.hypothesis_description: The use of the legacy `.transfer` method prevents smart contracts with complex receive logic from withdrawing their WAVAX.
threats.investigation_rationale: `WAVAX.withdraw` uses `payable(msg.sender).transfer(wad)`, which forwards a strict 2300 gas stipend. Any smart contract wallet or multisig that consumes more than 2300 gas in its fallback/receive function will be permanently unable to unwrap its WAVAX.
preconditions: A smart contract with a complex receive function holds WAVAX

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Use of deprecated .transfer method for ETH/AVAX transfers

operational_questions: Are protocol multisigs or operational contracts holding WAVAX?
Can they successfully unwrap it?
validation_requirements: Test WAVAX withdrawal to a contract that consumes > 2300 gas
Verify out-of-gas revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Smart contract wallet deposits AVAX to get WAVAX
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transaction reverts due to 2300 gas limit
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Funds are locked in WAVAX contract

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires transferring WAVAX to an EOA first

threats.title: GlobalRouter.exactInput is Completely Commented Out
threats.hypothesis_description: The exactInput function is entirely commented out, silently returning 0 and potentially causing fund loss for integrators.
threats.investigation_rationale: The body of `GlobalRouter.exactInput` consists only of commented-out code. It accepts payable ETH/tokens but performs no swaps and returns 0. Integrators calling this function will experience silent failures and potential loss of pre-transferred funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Function body is entirely commented out

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Dead code exposed as a public endpoint

operational_questions: Is this contract actively used in production?
Are frontends routing transactions through this endpoint?
validation_requirements: Test calling exactInput with valid parameters
Verify it returns 0 and does nothing

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User sends tokens and calls exactInput
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Function executes successfully but does nothing
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Input tokens are stuck in GlobalRouter or lost

threats.title: Stale Price Exploitation via Asymmetric Pause Controls
threats.hypothesis_description: The protocol pause mechanism restricts swaps but allows liquidity provision, enabling attackers to exploit stale prices during emergencies.
threats.investigation_rationale: `Pair.swap` checks `PairFactory(factory).isPaused()`, but `mint` and `burn` do not. If the protocol is paused during a period of high volatility, an attacker can add liquidity at the stale pool ratio. When the protocol unpauses, they can extract value by swapping against their own liquidity at the corrected market price.

proof_of_signal.pattern: swap has pause check, mint/burn do not

preconditions: Significant market price movement occurs during the pause

initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires admin to pause the protocol

operational_questions: What is the operational procedure for pausing?
Are LPs aware they can be diluted during a pause?
validation_requirements: Test minting and burning while protocol is paused
Simulate price movement and unpause exploitation

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin pauses protocol due to market event
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker adds one-sided liquidity at stale pool ratio
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker swaps against pool to extract value from stale ratio

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Loss of funds for existing LPs

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large mint/burn events while protocol is paused

threats.title: K-Invariant Reverts in Fee-On-Transfer Swaps
threats.hypothesis_description: Mismatched pool selection between amount calculation and swap execution causes K-invariant failures for fee-on-transfer tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: `RouterV2._swapSupportingFeeOnTransferTokens` uses `getAmountOut` to calculate the expected output, which returns the best amount across both stable and volatile pools. However, the swap is executed on the pool strictly specified by `routes[i].stable`. If the best pool differs from the specified pool, the swap will fail the K-invariant check.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Uses generic getAmountOut instead of pool-specific calculation

preconditions: Both stable and volatile pools exist for the pair
The best pool is different from the one specified in the route

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Inconsistent pool targeting between quote and execution
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: When liquidity distribution favors the non-routed pool

operational_questions: How often do users route FOT swaps through suboptimal pools?
Does this cause widespread transaction failures?
validation_requirements: Test FOT swap where volatile pool is better but route specifies stable
Verify K-invariant revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Router calculates amountOut using the better pool
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Router executes swap on the worse pool with the better amount
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Pair contract reverts due to K-invariant failure

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - user can retry with different route

threats.title: Zero Address Validation Bypass in GaugeV2.setInternalBribe
threats.hypothesis_description: A tautological validation check allows the internal bribe address to be set to the zero address, breaking fee distribution.
threats.investigation_rationale: The validation `require(_int >= address(0), "ZA");` is always true. This allows an admin to accidentally set `internal_bribe` to `address(0)`. Subsequent calls to `claimFees` will attempt to approve and notify the zero address, causing the transaction to revert and locking LP fees.
deployment_assumptions: Admin misconfigures the internal bribe address
threats.config_evidence: No deployment script evidence of this specific misconfiguration, but the code allows it.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Tautological require statement provides no actual validation

operational_questions: Is there off-chain validation before admin transactions are signed?
How quickly can the admin correct this if it happens?
validation_requirements: Test setting internal_bribe to address(0)
Verify claimFees reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin accidentally sets internal_bribe to address(0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transaction reverts when calling notifyRewardAmount on address(0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Fees are locked until admin fixes the address

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - admin can set a valid address
